examples.explanation: Total sum = 6. Recursion tree: Start dfs(0,0). i=0: include 1 -> dfs(1,1); exclude -> dfs(1,0). i=1 for dfs(1,1): include 2 -> dfs(2,3); exclude -> dfs(2,1). i=1 for dfs(1,0): include 2 -> dfs(2,2); exclude -> dfs(2,0). i=2 for dfs(2,3): include 3 -> dfs(3,6); exclude -> dfs(3,3). i=2 for dfs(2,1): include 3 -> dfs(3,4); exclude -> dfs(3,1). i=2 for dfs(2,2): include 3 -> dfs(3,5); exclude -> dfs(3,2). i=2 for dfs(2,0): include 3 -> dfs(3,3); exclude -> dfs(3,0). At i=3 (base): Check each: s=6, diff=|6-0|=6≠0; s=3, diff=|3-3|=0→count=1; s=4, diff=|4-2|=2≠0; s=1, diff=|1-5|=4≠0; s=5, diff=|5-1|=4≠0; s=2, diff=|2-4|=2≠0; s=3, diff=|3-3|=0→count=2; s=0, diff=|0-6|=6≠0. Total count=2.

Total sum = 3. Recursion: dfs(0,0). i=0: include 1 -> dfs(1,1); exclude -> dfs(1,0). i=1 for dfs(1,1): include 1 -> dfs(2,2); exclude -> dfs(2,1). i=1 for dfs(1,0): include 1 -> dfs(2,1); exclude -> dfs(2,0). i=2 for dfs(2,2): include 1 -> dfs(3,3); exclude -> dfs(3,2). i=2 for dfs(2,1): include 1 -> dfs(3,2); exclude -> dfs(3,1). i=2 for dfs(2,1): include 1 -> dfs(3,2); exclude -> dfs(3,1). i=2 for dfs(2,0): include 1 -> dfs(3,1); exclude -> dfs(3,0). Base i=3: s=3, diff=|3-0|=3≠1; s=2, diff=|2-1|=1→count=1; s=2, diff=|2-1|=1→count=2; s=1, diff=|1-2|=1→count=3; s=2, diff=|2-1|=1→count=4 (but note duplicate calls: actually 3 unique partitions: subsets {1}, {1}, {1} each give diff=1, so count=3).

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible subsets of the array recursively, compute the sum difference between the two partitions for each subset, and count those matching the given difference. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Partitions with Given Difference via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting partitions where subset sum difference equals target using recursive subset generation.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires counting ways to partition an array into two subsets such that the absolute difference between their sums equals a given target difference. The brute force approach explores all possible subsets by recursively deciding for each element whether to include it in the first subset (S1) or not (meaning it goes to S2). For each complete subset assignment, we compute sum(S1) - sum(S2) = target. Since sum(S2) = totalSum - sum(S1), the condition simplifies to sum(S1) = (target + totalSum)/2, provided this value is non-negative and integer. However, in brute force, we directly compute the difference without this transformation to maintain clarity. The recursion tree has 2^n leaves, each corresponding to a subset. At each leaf, we check if the difference matches target. The intuition is exhaustive search: we cannot miss any partition because we try all combinations. Step-by-step: 1. Compute total sum of array. 2. Define recursive function dfs(i, currentSum) where i is current index and currentSum is sum of elements chosen for S1 so far. 3. Base case: if i == n, check if |currentSum - (totalSum - currentSum)| == target, increment count if true. 4. Recursive case: two calls—include arr[i] in S1 (currentSum + arr[i]) or exclude it (currentSum unchanged). 5. Initiate dfs(0, 0). Time: O(2^n) from 2^n recursive calls. Space: O(n) recursion stack depth. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive subset generation subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling negative differences (use absolute value), missing base case, or incorrect index handling. Real code pattern: def countPartitions(arr, target): total = sum(arr); count = 0; def dfs(i, s): if i == len(arr): if abs(s - (total - s)) == target: count += 1; return; dfs(i+1, s + arr[i]); dfs(i+1, s); dfs(0,0); return count.

examples.trace: Total sum = 6. Recursion tree: Start dfs(0,0). i=0: include 1 -> dfs(1,1); exclude -> dfs(1,0). i=1 for dfs(1,1): include 2 -> dfs(2,3); exclude -> dfs(2,1). i=1 for dfs(1,0): include 2 -> dfs(2,2); exclude -> dfs(2,0). i=2 for dfs(2,3): include 3 -> dfs(3,6); exclude -> dfs(3,3). i=2 for dfs(2,1): include 3 -> dfs(3,4); exclude -> dfs(3,1). i=2 for dfs(2,2): include 3 -> dfs(3,5); exclude -> dfs(3,2). i=2 for dfs(2,0): include 3 -> dfs(3,3); exclude -> dfs(3,0). At i=3 (base): Check each: s=6, diff=|6-0|=6≠0; s=3, diff=|3-3|=0→count=1; s=4, diff=|4-2|=2≠0; s=1, diff=|1-5|=4≠0; s=5, diff=|5-1|=4≠0; s=2, diff=|2-4|=2≠0; s=3, diff=|3-3|=0→count=2; s=0, diff=|0-6|=6≠0. Total count=2.

Total sum = 3. Recursion: dfs(0,0). i=0: include 1 -> dfs(1,1); exclude -> dfs(1,0). i=1 for dfs(1,1): include 1 -> dfs(2,2); exclude -> dfs(2,1). i=1 for dfs(1,0): include 1 -> dfs(2,1); exclude -> dfs(2,0). i=2 for dfs(2,2): include 1 -> dfs(3,3); exclude -> dfs(3,2). i=2 for dfs(2,1): include 1 -> dfs(3,2); exclude -> dfs(3,1). i=2 for dfs(2,1): include 1 -> dfs(3,2); exclude -> dfs(3,1). i=2 for dfs(2,0): include 1 -> dfs(3,1); exclude -> dfs(3,0). Base i=3: s=3, diff=|3-0|=3≠1; s=2, diff=|2-1|=1→count=1; s=2, diff=|2-1|=1→count=2; s=1, diff=|1-2|=1→count=3; s=2, diff=|2-1|=1→count=4 (but note duplicate calls: actually 3 unique partitions: subsets {1}, {1}, {1} each give diff=1, so count=3).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subset Sum Generation
ladders.desc: Generate all subsets of an array recursively and compute their sums.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of brute force: it recursively explores all 2^n subsets by deciding for each element whether to include it in the current subset or not. The intuition is binary choice per element, building a recursion tree where each path from root to leaf represents a unique subset. We maintain a running sum of included elements. At the base case (after processing all elements), we have the sum of a complete subset. This allows checking conditions (like difference matching) in the full problem. Step-by-step: 1. Define function dfs(i, currentSum) where i is index in array arr of length n, currentSum is sum of elements included so far. 2. Base: if i == n, we have a complete subset sum—process it (e.g., store or check). 3. Recursive: two calls—include arr[i] by adding to currentSum and increment i, or exclude arr[i] by keeping currentSum unchanged and increment i. 4. Initiate with dfs(0, 0). Time: O(2^n) as 2^n leaves. Space: O(n) recursion depth. Connection to L0: This generates subsets whose sums are used to compute partition differences. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in index, not resetting variables, or misunderstanding recursion flow. Real code pattern: def generateSubsets(arr): subsets = []; def dfs(i, s): if i == len(arr): subsets.append(s); return; dfs(i+1, s + arr[i]); dfs(i+1, s); dfs(0,0); return subsets.

examples.trace: Start dfs(0,0). i=0: include 1 -> dfs(1,1); exclude -> dfs(1,0). i=1 for dfs(1,1): include 2 -> dfs(2,3); exclude -> dfs(2,1). i=1 for dfs(1,0): include 2 -> dfs(2,2); exclude -> dfs(2,0). At i=2 (base): process sums: 3,1,2,0. Order may vary, but all subsets: {} sum=0, {2} sum=2, {1} sum=1, {1,2} sum=3.
Start dfs(0,0). i=0: include 3 -> dfs(1,3); exclude -> dfs(1,0). At i=1: process sums: 3 and 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Partition Difference from Subset Sum
ladders.desc: Calculate the absolute difference between two subset sums given total sum and one subset sum.
ladders.explanation: Given an array partitioned into two subsets S1 and S2, with total sum T = sum(arr), and sum of S1 = S, the difference D = |S - (T - S)| = |2S - T|. This formula is key because it reduces the problem to finding subsets with sum S such that |2S - T| equals target. In brute force, we compute D directly during recursion. The intuition: partition difference depends only on S1's sum since S2's sum is T - S. By iterating over all possible S (via subset sums), we can check D. Step-by-step: 1. Compute T = sum(arr). 2. For each candidate sum S from subset generation, compute D = |2*S - T|. 3. Check if D == target. This avoids needing to track both subsets explicitly. Time: O(1) per subset. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is applied to each generated subset sum. Common mistakes: Forgetting absolute value, miscalculating T, or not handling integer division if transforming. Real code pattern: def computeDifference(S, total): return abs(2*S - total).

examples.trace: D = |2*3 - 6| = |6-6| = 0. Since 0 == target, it matches.
D = |2*4 - 10| = |8-10| = 2. Since 2 == target, it matches (output should be yes, but example shows no—correction: it matches).

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal with State
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary recursion tree while maintaining a state variable (like current sum).
ladders.explanation: This concept involves using recursion to explore all paths in a binary tree, where each node represents a decision (include/exclude an element), and we carry state (e.g., current sum) down the recursion. The intuition: recursion naturally implements depth-first search (DFS) on an implicit tree. At each step, we update state based on the decision, recurse, and backtrack implicitly via function returns. This is foundational for subset generation. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function dfs(i, state). 2. Base case: if i == n, process state (e.g., record it). 3. Recursive case: update state for one branch (e.g., state + arr[i]), call dfs(i+1, updated state), then call dfs(i+1, original state) for the other branch. 4. Initiate with initial state. Time: O(2^n) for full traversal. Space: O(n) for call stack. Connection to L2: This traversal generates states (sums) used in difference computation. Common mistakes: Modifying state in place without copying, incorrect base condition, or infinite recursion. Real code pattern: def traverse(i, s): if i == n: process(s); return; traverse(i+1, s + arr[i]); traverse(i+1, s).

examples.trace: Tree: root i=0,s=0. Left child include 1: i=1,s=1. Left leaf include 2: i=2,s=3→process 3. Right leaf exclude 2: i=2,s=1→process 1. Right child exclude 1: i=1,s=0. Left leaf include 2: i=2,s=2→process 2. Right leaf exclude 2: i=2,s=0→process 0.
Root i=0,s=0. Left include 5: i=1,s=5→process 5. Right exclude: i=1,s=0→process 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Inclusion/Exclusion in Subset
ladders.desc: Decide for a single element whether to include it in a subset or not, updating sum accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation in subset generation: for each element in the array, we make a binary choice—include it in the current subset or exclude it. If included, we add its value to the running sum; if excluded, the sum remains unchanged. The intuition: each element independently contributes or not to the subset, leading to 2^n total combinations. This operation is performed recursively for all elements. Step-by-step: 1. For element at index i with value arr[i], current sum is s. 2. Inclusion: new sum = s + arr[i]. 3. Exclusion: new sum = s. 4. Proceed to next element with the new sum. Time: O(1) per decision. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is repeated at each node in the recursion tree. Common mistakes: Adding incorrectly, confusing index, or not handling zero values. Real code pattern: include_sum = s + arr[i]; exclude_sum = s.

examples.trace: Current sum s=5. For element value 3: if include, new sum = 5+3=8; if exclude, new sum remains 5.
s=0, value 0: include gives 0+0=0; exclude gives 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summation of Numbers
ladders.desc: Add a sequence of numbers to compute a total sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: summing a list of numbers. In the partition problem, we need the total sum of the array to compute differences. Summation is a basic arithmetic operation where we iterate through each number and accumulate their values. Intuition: the total sum represents the combined weight of all elements, which is constant for a given array and used to derive subset sums. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize total = 0. 2. For each number in the array, add it to total. 3. Return total. Time: O(n) for n numbers. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Summation is used to compute initial total and update running sums during inclusion. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize total, incorrect loop bounds, or overflow with large numbers. Real code pattern: total = 0; for num in arr: total += num.

examples.trace: Start total=0. Add 1: total=1. Add 2: total=3. Add 3: total=6.
total=0. Add 5: total=5. Add 5: total=10.

approaches.name: DP Subset Sum Transformation O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Transform the problem into a subset sum count by noting that the difference condition can be expressed as finding subsets with a specific target sum. Use dynamic programming with a 1D or 2D array to count subsets achieving this target, leveraging the subset sum problem structure.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * sum) where sum is total array sum
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * sum) or O(sum) with optimization

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Partitions with Given Difference Using DP Subset Sum
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting partitions where difference between subset sums equals given target using subset sum transformation.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires counting partitions of an array into two subsets S1 and S2 such that sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d (given difference). This can be transformed into a subset sum counting problem. Let total sum = total. We have sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d and sum(S1) + sum(S2) = total. Solving these equations gives sum(S1) = (total + d)/2. So we need to count subsets with sum = (total + d)/2. This must be a non-negative integer. We use DP where dp[i][s] = number of subsets using first i elements with sum s. Base case: dp[0][0] = 1 (empty subset). For each element arr[i-1], we either exclude it (dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s]) or include it (dp[i][s] += dp[i-1][s-arr[i-1]] if s >= arr[i-1]). The answer is dp[n][target] where target = (total + d)/2. Time: O(n * total) where total is array sum. Space: O(n * total) for 2D DP, optimizable to O(total) with 1D DP. Common mistakes: Not handling negative d or when (total + d) is odd/negative. Ensure target is non-negative integer and use modulo if needed.

examples.trace:
total = 7, target = (7+1)/2 = 4. Need subsets with sum 4. DP table (rows: elements, cols: sums 0-4):
Initialize dp[0][0]=1, others 0.
Element 1: dp[1][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1.
Element 1: dp[2][0]=1, dp[2][1]=2, dp[2][2]=1.
Element 2: dp[3][0]=1, dp[3][1]=2, dp[3][2]=2, dp[3][3]=1, dp[3][4]=1.
Element 3: dp[4][0]=1, dp[4][1]=2, dp[4][2]=2, dp[4][3]=2, dp[4][4]=3.
Answer: dp[4][4]=3. Subsets: {1,3}, {1,3} (two 1's), {1,1,2}.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Subsets with Given Sum Using DP
ladders.desc: Count number of subsets in an array that sum to a target value using dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine solves the subset sum counting problem: given an array of non-negative integers and a target sum, count how many subsets have exactly that sum. We use a DP table dp[i][s] where i ranges from 0 to n (number of elements) and s ranges from 0 to target. dp[i][s] represents the number of subsets using the first i elements that sum to s. Base case: dp[0][0] = 1 (empty subset sums to 0), and dp[0][s] = 0 for s>0. For each element arr[i-1] (1-indexed in DP), we have two choices: exclude the element, so dp[i][s] inherits dp[i-1][s]; or include the element, so we add dp[i-1][s-arr[i-1]] if s >= arr[i-1]. Thus, dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] + (dp[i-1][s-arr[i-1]] if s >= arr[i-1] else 0). The answer is dp[n][target]. Time complexity: O(n * target). Space: O(n * target), optimizable to O(target) using a 1D DP array updated backwards. This feeds into L0 by providing the core counting mechanism after transforming the partition problem. Common mistakes: Not initializing base case correctly, or using forward update in 1D DP which counts combinations multiple times.

examples.trace:
DP table (rows: elements, cols: sums 0-3):
Initialize dp[0][0]=1, others 0.
Element 1: dp[1][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1.
Element 2: dp[2][0]=1, dp[2][1]=1, dp[2][2]=1, dp[2][3]=1.
Element 3: dp[3][0]=1, dp[3][1]=1, dp[3][2]=1, dp[3][3]=2.
Answer: dp[3][3]=2. Subsets: {3}, {1,2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Transforming Partition Difference to Subset Sum
ladders.desc: Derive the mathematical transformation from partition difference condition to subset sum target.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is converting the partition difference problem into a subset sum counting problem using algebra. Given an array, we want to partition it into two subsets S1 and S2 such that sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d, where d is the given difference. Let total = sum of all elements. We have two equations: (1) sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d, and (2) sum(S1) + sum(S2) = total (since every element goes into either S1 or S2). Add these equations: 2*sum(S1) = total + d, so sum(S1) = (total + d)/2. Similarly, subtract: 2*sum(S2) = total - d, so sum(S2) = (total - d)/2. Thus, the problem reduces to counting subsets with sum = (total + d)/2 (or equivalently (total - d)/2). Key conditions: (total + d) must be non-negative and even for sum(S1) to be a non-negative integer. If not, answer is 0. This transformation works because any subset S1 with sum(S1) = (total + d)/2 automatically gives S2 as the complement with sum(S2) = total - sum(S1) = (total - d)/2, satisfying the difference condition. This feeds into L1 by providing the target for the subset sum counting. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check if (total + d) is even or negative, or misunderstanding that we count subsets for S1 (which determines S2 uniquely).

examples.trace: total = 1+1+2+3 = 7. sum(S1) = (7+1)/2 = 8/2 = 4. Check: 7+1=8 is even and non-negative. So we need subsets with sum 4.
total = 10. sum(S1) = (10+2)/2 = 12/2 = 6. Check: 10+2=12 is even and non-negative. So target sum is 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming for Counting Problems
ladders.desc: Understand DP for counting combinations with inclusion/exclusion choices.
ladders.explanation: Dynamic programming (DP) for counting problems involves building a table where each entry represents the count of ways to achieve a certain state. For subset-related problems, we define dp[i][s] as the number of ways to achieve sum s using the first i elements. The recurrence comes from considering the last element: either we exclude it, so the count is dp[i-1][s]; or we include it, so we add dp[i-1][s-arr[i-1]] if the element's value does not exceed s. This is a classic 0/1 knapsack counting variant. Intuition: We are counting combinations, not just existence, so we sum possibilities. Base case: dp[0][0] = 1 (one way to make sum 0 with no elements: empty subset). dp[0][s] = 0 for s>0. Time: O(n * S) where S is max sum. Space: O(n * S). This feeds into L2 by providing the DP framework to count subsets after transformation. Common mistakes: Confusing with optimization DP (max/min), not initializing base case correctly, or using += incorrectly in updates.

examples.input: arr = [1,2], count subsets with various sums
examples.trace:
Let target sums be 0,1,2,3. Initialize dp[0][0]=1.
After element 1: dp[1][0]=1 (exclude), dp[1][1]=1 (include).
After element 2: dp[2][0]=1 (exclude both), dp[2][1]=1 (include only 1), dp[2][2]=1 (include only 2), dp[2][3]=1 (include both). So counts: sum0:1, sum1:1, sum2:1, sum3:1.

Initialize dp[0][0]=1.
Element 2: dp[1][0]=1, dp[1][2]=1.
Element 3: dp[2][0]=1, dp[2][2]=1, dp[2][3]=1, dp[2][5]=1 (include both 2 and 3). So dp[2][5]=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Array and Updating Counts
ladders.desc: Process each array element to update counts for possible sums using loops.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves scanning through the array and for each element, updating a list of counts for all possible sums. We maintain an array 'count' where count[s] stores the number of ways to achieve sum s so far. Initially, count[0] = 1 (empty subset), others 0. For each element x in the array, we iterate over sums from high to low (to avoid reusing the same element multiple times in one subset) and update: for s from maxSum down to x: count[s] += count[s-x]. This means if we have count[s-x] ways to make sum s-x, adding element x gives count[s-x] new ways to make sum s. The high-to-low iteration ensures that when we process count[s], count[s-x] hasn't been updated yet in this round (so it reflects counts without the current element). This is a fundamental DP update pattern. Time per element: O(maxSum). This feeds into L3 by implementing the DP recurrence efficiently. Common mistakes: Iterating from low to high (which counts combinations with multiple uses of same element), or not initializing count[0]=1.

examples.trace:
Initialize count = [1,0,0,0] for sums 0-3.
Element 1: iterate s from 3 down to 1. s=3: count[3] += count[2]=0. s=2: count[2] += count[1]=0. s=1: count[1] += count[0]=1 → count[1]=1. Now count = [1,1,0,0].
Element 2: iterate s from 3 down to 2. s=3: count[3] += count[1]=1 → count[3]=1. s=2: count[2] += count[0]=1 → count[2]=1. Final count = [1,1,1,1].

Initialize count = [1,0,0].
Element 2: iterate s from 2 down to 2. s=2: count[2] += count[0]=1 → count[2]=1. Final count = [1,0,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subsets and Sums of Arrays
ladders.desc: Understand what subsets are and how to compute sums of subsets.
ladders.explanation: A subset of an array is a selection of elements where order doesn't matter, and each element can be either included or excluded. For an array of size n, there are 2^n possible subsets (including the empty subset). The sum of a subset is the total of all elements in that subset. For example, array [1,2] has subsets: {} (sum 0), {1} (sum 1), {2} (sum 2), {1,2} (sum 3). This is foundational because the partition problem involves dividing the array into two subsets. To work with subsets programmatically, we often use bitmasking or iterative DP, but at this level, focus on the conceptual understanding. Key intuition: Every element has two choices (in or out), leading to exponential possibilities. Sums can range from 0 (empty subset) to total sum of array (all elements). This feeds into L4 by providing the basic idea of enumerating subsets via inclusion/exclusion. Common mistakes: Confusing subsets with subarrays (which are contiguous), or forgetting the empty subset.

examples.trace: Subsets: {} → sum 0, {1} → sum 1, {2} → sum 2, {1,2} → sum 3. Total subsets: 4 = 2^2.
Subsets: {} → sum 0, {3} → sum 3. Total subsets: 2 = 2^1.

approaches.name: DP with Modular Optimization O(n * sum)
approaches.summary: Similar to the subset sum DP, but optimize by handling negative sums or large ranges using modular arithmetic or offset indices to map sums to non-negative indices, ensuring correctness when array elements can be negative or zero.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Partitions with Given Difference using DP with Modular Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting partitions where difference between subset sums equals target, handling negative numbers via modular arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting partitions of an array into two subsets S1 and S2 such that sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d (given difference). We transform this to find subsets with sum = (total + d)/2, where total is sum of all elements. The challenge arises when array contains negative numbers or zero, making standard DP indices negative. We solve this using modular arithmetic with an offset to map all possible sums to non-negative indices. Let total_sum = sum(arr). We need to find count of subsets with sum s = (total_sum + d)/2. First, check if (total_sum + d) is even and non-negative; otherwise return 0. Define DP[i][j] = number of subsets using first i elements with sum j (after offset). Since sums can range from -total_sum to total_sum, we use offset = total_sum to map sum j to index j + offset. Initialize DP[0][offset] = 1 (empty subset sum 0). For each element arr[i-1], update DP[i][j] = DP[i-1][j] + DP[i-1][j - arr[i-1]] if j - arr[i-1] is within bounds. Finally, answer is DP[n][s + offset] if s is valid. Time O(n * (2*total_sum+1)) = O(n * sum), space O(n * sum) reducible to O(sum) using 1D DP. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into final solution. Common mistakes: Not handling negative sums in DP indices, forgetting offset adjustment, missing parity check. Real code pattern: Use 2D list with dimensions (n+1) x (2*total_sum+1), iterate i from 1 to n, j from 0 to 2*total_sum, update based on element value.

examples.trace: total_sum = 0, s = (0+0)/2 = 0, offset = 0, DP size 4x1. Initialize DP[0][0] = 1. i=1, arr[0]=1: DP[1][0] = DP[0][0] + (DP[0][-1] invalid) = 1 (exclude). DP[1][1] would be out of bounds since max index 0, so ignore. Actually with offset 0, sums range -0 to 0, so only index 0. Thus DP[1][0]=1. i=2, arr[1]=-1: DP[2][0] = DP[1][0] + (DP[1][1] invalid) = 1. i=3, arr[2]=0: DP[3][0] = DP[2][0] + DP[2][0] = 2. But wait, we need to handle zero separately: For zero elements, DP[i][j] = DP[i-1][j] + DP[i-1][j] = 2*DP[i-1][j]. So DP[3][0] = 2*DP[2][0] = 2*1 = 2. However, total partitions: subsets with sum 0: {}, {0}, {-1,1}, {-1,1,0} = 4. Let's recompute with correct zero handling: Initialize DP[0][0]=1. i=1, element 1: DP[1][0] = DP[0][0] = 1 (exclude), DP[1][1] = DP[0][0] = 1 (include). i=2, element -1: DP[2][0] = DP[1][0] + DP[1][1] = 1+1=2, DP[2][1] = DP[1][1] + DP[1][2] (invalid) = 1. i=3, element 0: For zero, DP[3][j] = 2*DP[2][j]. So DP[3][0] = 2*2=4, DP[3][1] = 2*1=2. s=0, answer = DP[3][0] = 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP for Subset Sum Count with Negative Numbers using Offset
ladders.desc: Count subsets with given sum, handling negative elements by shifting DP indices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine solves: Given array arr (may contain negatives) and target sum s, count subsets of arr with sum exactly s. Standard subset sum DP uses indices 0 to total_positive_sum, but negatives allow sums from -total_abs_sum to total_abs_sum. We map each possible sum to a non-negative index using offset = total_abs_sum, where total_abs_sum = sum(abs(x) for x in arr). Then DP[i][j] = number of subsets using first i elements with sum = j - offset. Initialize DP[0][offset] = 1 (empty subset sum 0). For each element arr[i-1], update: DP[i][j] = DP[i-1][j] + DP[i-1][j - arr[i-1]] if 0 ≤ j - arr[i-1] ≤ 2*total_abs_sum. Handle zero elements specially: if arr[i-1] == 0, DP[i][j] = 2 * DP[i-1][j] because including zero doesn't change sum. Answer is DP[n][s + offset] if s within bounds. Time O(n * (2*total_abs_sum+1)), space O(n * total_abs_sum). Connection to L0: This directly computes the transformed sum s = (total + d)/2 in the full problem. Intuition: Offset acts as translation to avoid negative indices, preserving DP correctness. Common mistakes: Using total_sum instead of total_abs_sum for offset, incorrect bounds checking, mishandling zeros. Real code pattern: Create DP table with n+1 rows and 2*total_abs_sum+1 columns, iterate elements, update with conditionals.

examples.trace: total_abs_sum = 8, offset = 8, DP size 4x17. Initialize DP[0][8]=1. i=1, arr[0]=0: DP[1][j] = 2*DP[0][j]. So DP[1][8]=2. i=2, arr[1]=5: DP[2][j] = DP[1][j] + (DP[1][j-5] if 0≤j-5≤16). DP[2][8] = DP[1][8] + DP[1][3] = 2+0=2, DP[2][13] = DP[1][13] + DP[1][8] = 0+2=2. i=3, arr[2]=-3: DP[3][j] = DP[2][j] + (DP[2][j+3] if 0≤j+3≤16). DP[3][10] = DP[2][10] + DP[2][13] = 0+2=2? Wait, target s=2, index = 2+8=10. Compute DP[3][10]: DP[2][10]=0, DP[2][13]=2, so DP[3][10]=0+2=2? But let's trace carefully: For j=10, DP[3][10] = DP[2][10] + DP[2][10 - (-3)] = DP[2][10] + DP[2][13] = 0 + 2 = 2. However, subsets with sum 2: {5, -3} only, so count should be 1. Error: Because zero element doubled counts incorrectly. Actually with zero, number of subsets doubles for each zero. Let's recompute: After i=1, DP[1][8]=2 represents subsets {} and {0}. i=2, element 5: DP[2][8] = DP[1][8] + DP[1][3] = 2+0=2 (subsets without 5: {} and {0}), DP[2][13] = DP[1][13] + DP[1][8] = 0+2=2 (subsets with 5: {5} and {0,5}). i=3, element -3: DP[3][10] = DP[2][10] + DP[2][13] = 0+2=2. But subsets with sum 2: {5, -3} and {0,5,-3}. Yes, that's 2. Wait, target s=2, subsets: {5, -3} sum=2, {0,5,-3} sum=2. So answer should be 2. But earlier I said 1, incorrect. Let's verify: arr=[0,5,-3], subsets: {} sum0, {0} sum0, {5} sum5, {0,5} sum5, {-3} sum-3, {0,-3} sum-3, {5,-3} sum2, {0,5,-3} sum2. So count for sum2 is 2. Thus output 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Transforming Partition Difference to Subset Sum
ladders.desc: Derive subset sum target from partition difference constraint using algebraic manipulation.
ladders.explanation: The core insight converts the partition difference problem into a subset sum problem. Given array arr, we need count of partitions into subsets S1 and S2 such that sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d. Let total = sum(arr). Since sum(S1) + sum(S2) = total, we have two equations: (1) sum(S1) - sum(S2) = d, (2) sum(S1) + sum(S2) = total. Adding (1) and (2): 2*sum(S1) = total + d => sum(S1) = (total + d)/2. Similarly, sum(S2) = (total - d)/2. Thus, the problem reduces to counting subsets with sum exactly s = (total + d)/2. Note: s must be integer and non-negative, so (total + d) must be even and ≥ 0. If not, answer is 0. This transformation is crucial because subset sum is a well-studied DP problem. Intuition: The difference constraint fixes one subset's sum once total is known. Connection to L1: This target s is input to the subset sum counting subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check parity of (total+d), not ensuring s is non-negative, misunderstanding that S1 and S2 are not ordered (so counting subsets directly works). Real code pattern: Compute total, check if (total+d)%2 != 0 or total+d < 0, return 0; else set target = (total+d)//2.

examples.trace: total = 10, total + d = 15, which is odd, so cannot have integer sum(S1) = 15/2 = 7.5. Thus immediately return 0 without DP.
total = 17, total + d = 20, even, so s = 20/2 = 10. Now find subsets with sum 10: only {6,4} works. So answer 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Subset Sum Counting DP (Positive Numbers Only)
ladders.desc: Count number of subsets with given sum using dynamic programming, assuming all elements are non-negative.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept solves: Given array arr of non-negative integers and target sum s, count subsets of arr with sum exactly s. Use DP where dp[i][j] = number of subsets using first i elements with sum j. Initialize dp[0][0] = 1 (empty subset sum 0), dp[0][j>0] = 0. For each element arr[i-1] from i=1 to n, update: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + (dp[i-1][j - arr[i-1]] if j ≥ arr[i-1]). This means either exclude the element (keep count from previous) or include it (add count from sum reduced by element's value). Handle zero elements: if arr[i-1] == 0, then including zero doesn't change sum, so dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i-1][j] = 2 * dp[i-1][j]. Answer is dp[n][s]. Time O(n * s), space O(n * s) reducible to O(s) using 1D DP. Intuition: DP builds solutions incrementally, combining counts from smaller subproblems. Connection to L2: This is the DP used after transformation, but needs extension for negative numbers. Common mistakes: Not initializing dp[0][0]=1, incorrect recurrence for zero, off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: Create 2D list with dimensions (n+1) x (s+1), nested loops over i and j, apply recurrence.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative DP Table Update for Inclusion/Exclusion
ladders.desc: Update DP table for one element using include/exclude choices, fundamental to subset problems.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves updating a DP array when considering a new element. Given current DP array dp_prev representing counts for sums without the element, and element value val, produce new array dp_curr where dp_curr[sum] = dp_prev[sum] + dp_prev[sum - val] (if sum ≥ val). This captures two choices: exclude the element (keep count from dp_prev) or include it (add count from dp_prev at sum reduced by val). For zero val, dp_curr[sum] = 2 * dp_prev[sum] because including zero doubles each count without changing sum. Operation must be done carefully to avoid overwriting values needed later; typically iterate sums in reverse when using 1D DP to prevent using updated values within same iteration. Time O(S) where S is range of sums, space O(S). Intuition: Each element offers a binary choice, and DP aggregates counts across all combinations. Connection to L3: This operation is repeated for each element in subset sum DP. Common mistakes: Updating in forward order causing multiple uses of same element, missing zero handling, index out of bounds. Real code pattern: For val > 0: for sum from max_sum down to val: dp[sum] += dp[sum - val]. For val == 0: for all sum: dp[sum] *= 2.

examples.trace: Initialize dp_curr as copy of dp_prev: [1,1,0,0,0]. For sum from 4 down to 2: sum=4: dp_curr[4] += dp_prev[4-2] = 0 + dp_prev[2] = 0+0=0. sum=3: dp_curr[3] += dp_prev[1] = 0+1=1. sum=2: dp_curr[2] += dp_prev[0] = 0+1=1. Result dp_curr = [1,1,1,1,0]. Interpretation: dp_prev counts subsets without element 2: sums 0 and 1 each count 1. After including 2, new subsets: sum 2 from {2}, sum 3 from {1,2}.
For zero element, double each count: dp_curr[0] = 2*2=4, dp_curr[1]=2*0=0, dp_curr[2]=2*1=2, dp_curr[3]=2*0=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subset Sum Problem and Dynamic Programming Introduction
ladders.desc: Understand subset sum decision problem and basic DP concept for beginners.
ladders.explanation: The subset sum problem asks: Given a set of integers and a target sum, is there a subset that sums to the target? This is a foundational NP-complete problem, but for small sums solvable via DP. Dynamic programming (DP) solves it by breaking into overlapping subproblems. Define dp[i][j] as boolean: can we achieve sum j using first i elements? Base case: dp[0][0] = true (empty subset sum 0), dp[0][j>0] = false. Recurrence: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR dp[i-1][j - arr[i-1]] if j ≥ arr[i-1]. This means we can achieve sum j either by excluding the i-th element (dp[i-1][j]) or including it (dp[i-1][j - arr[i-1]]). Answer is dp[n][target]. Intuition: Build table row by row, each cell depends on previous row, exploring all combinations without recursion. Time O(n * target), space O(n * target). Connection to L4: This boolean DP extends to counting by storing integers instead of booleans. Common mistakes: Confusing indices, not initializing base case, misunderstanding recurrence. Real code pattern: Create 2D boolean list, nested loops, use OR logic.

examples.trace: DP table size 4x8 (sums 0..7). Initialize dp[0][0]=true. i=1, arr[0]=3: dp[1][0]=true, dp[1][3]=true. i=2, arr[1]=4: dp[2][0]=true, dp[2][3]=true, dp[2][4]=true, dp[2][7]=true (since dp[1][3]=true). i=3, arr[2]=5: dp[3][0]=true, dp[3][3]=true, dp[3][4]=true, dp[3][7]=true, dp[3][8] out of range. dp[3][7] remains true. Answer true. Subset {3,4} sums to 7.

DP size 3x5. Initialize dp[0][0]=true. i=1, arr[0]=1: dp[1][0]=true, dp[1][1]=true. i=2, arr[1]=2: dp[2][0]=true, dp[2][1]=true, dp[2][2]=true, dp[2][3]=true (from dp[1][1]), dp[2][4]=false. Answer false.
